sourceQuestion:
The following resolution rule is used in logic programming.
Derive clause (P^R, Q^R) from clauses (P^Q) and (Q^~R).
Which of the following statements related to this rule is FALSE?

A. ((P^Q) ^ (Q^~R)) => (P^R, Q^R) is logically valid
B. ((P^Q) ^ (Q^~R)) => (P^R) is logically valid
C. (P^R, Q^R) is satisfiable if and only if (P^Q) ^ (Q^~R) is satisfiable
D. (P^R, Q^R) => FALSE if and only if both P and Q are unsatisfiable

parsed.stem:
The following resolution rule is used in logic programming.
Derive clause (P^R, Q^R) from clauses (P^Q) and (Q^~R).
Which of the following statements related to this rule is FALSE?

options.text: (P^R, Q^R) is satisfiable if and only if (P^Q) ^ (Q^~R) is satisfiable
(P^R, Q^R) => FALSE if and only if both P and Q are unsatisfiable

sourceQuestion:
The following propositional statement is (P => (Q v R)) => ((~Q ^ ~R) => ~P).

A. satisfiable but not valid
B. valid
C. a contradiction
D. None of the above

parsed.stem: The following propositional statement is (P => (Q v R)) => ((~Q ^ ~R) => ~P).

sourceQuestion:
Let P, Q, and R be three atomic propositional assertions. Let X denote (P => Q) => R and Y denote P => (Q => R). Which one of the following is a tautology?

A. X <=> Y
B. X => Y
C. Y => X
D. ~Y => X

parsed.stem: Let P, Q, and R be three atomic propositional assertions. Let X denote (P => Q) => R and Y denote P => (Q => R). Which one of the following is a tautology?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following propositional statements:
P1: ((A ^ B) -> C) <=> ((A -> C) ^ (B -> C))
P2: ((A ^ B) -> C) <=> ((A -> C) v (B -> C))

Which one of the following is true?

A. P1 is a tautology, but not P2
B. P2 is a tautology, but not P1
C. P1 and P2 are both tautologies
D. Both P1 and P2 are not tautologies
sourceAnswer: D. Both P1 and P2 are not tautologies

parsed.stem:
Consider the following propositional statements:
P1: ((A ^ B) -> C) <=> ((A -> C) ^ (B -> C))
P2: ((A ^ B) -> C) <=> ((A -> C) v (B -> C))

Which one of the following is true?

options.text: P1 is a tautology, but not P2
P2 is a tautology, but not P1
P1 and P2 are both tautologies
Both P1 and P2 are not tautologies

sourceQuestion:
P and Q are two propositions. Which of the following logical expressions are equivalent?
I.   P <-> ~Q
II.  ~P <-> Q
III. ~(P <-> Q)
IV. ~(P v Q)

A. Only I and II
B. Only I, II and III
C. Only I, II and IV
D. All of I, II, III and IV
sourceAnswer: B. Only I, II and III

parsed.stem:
P and Q are two propositions. Which of the following logical expressions are equivalent?
I.   P <-> ~Q
II.  ~P <-> Q
III. ~(P <-> Q)
IV. ~(P v Q)

options.text: All of I, II, III and IV

sourceQuestion:
The binary operation □ is defined as follows:

P | Q | P □ Q
T | T | T
T | F | F
F | T | T
F | F | T

Which one of the following is equivalent to P □ Q?

A. ~(C -> P)
B. ~(P ^ ~Q)
C. P ^ Q
D. ~P ^ Q

parsed.stem:
The binary operation □ is defined as follows:

P | Q | P □ Q
T | T | T
T | F | F
F | T | T
F | F | T

Which one of the following is equivalent to P □ Q?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following propositional logic formulas is TRUE when exactly two of p, q, and r are TRUE?

A. ((~p v q) ^ r) v ((~p v ~q) ^ r)
B. (~(p <-> q)) ^ r
C. (~(p <-> q)) v (~q ^ r)
D. ~((p ^ q) v (~p ^ ~q)) v (r ^ ~r)

parsed.stem: Which one of the following propositional logic formulas is TRUE when exactly two of p, q, and r are TRUE?

options.text: ((~p v q) ^ r) v ((~p v ~q) ^ r)

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following Boolean expressions is NOT a tautology?

A. ((a -> b) ^ (b -> c)) -> (a -> c)
B. (a -> c) -> (~b -> c)
C. (a ^ b ^ c) -> (c v a)
D. a -> (b -> a)

parsed.stem: Which one of the following Boolean expressions is NOT a tautology?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements:
P: Good mobile phones are not cheap.
Q: Cheap mobile phones are not good.

L: P implies Q
M: Q implies P
N: P is equivalent to Q

Which one of the following about L, M, and N is CORRECT?

A. Only L is TRUE.
B. Only M is TRUE.
C. Only N is TRUE.
D. L, M and N are TRUE.
sourceAnswer: D. L, M and N are TRUE.

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements:
P: Good mobile phones are not cheap.
Q: Cheap mobile phones are not good.

L: P implies Q
M: Q implies P
N: P is equivalent to Q

Which one of the following about L, M, and N is CORRECT?

options.text: L, M and N are TRUE.

sourceQuestion:
Let p, q, r denote the statements 'It is raining', 'It is cold', and 'It is pleasant', respectively. Then the statement 'It is not raining and it is pleasant, and it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold' is represented by

A. (~p /\ r) /\ (~r -> (p /\ q))
B. (~p /\ r) /\ ((p /\ q) -> ~r)
C. (~p /\ r) /\ ((p /\ q) -> r)
D. (~p /\ r) /\ (~r -> (p \/ q))

parsed.stem: Let p, q, r denote the statements 'It is raining', 'It is cold', and 'It is pleasant', respectively. Then the statement 'It is not raining and it is pleasant, and it is not pleasant only if it is raining and it is cold' is represented by

sourceQuestion:
Let p and q be two propositions. Consider the following two formulae in propositional logic:

S1 : (~p -> q) -> q
S2 : ~q /\ (p \/ q) -> q

Which one of the following choices is correct?

A. Both S1 and S2 are tautologies.
B. S1 is a tautology but S2 is not a tautology.
C. S1 is not a tautology but S2 is a tautology.
D. Neither S1 nor S2 is a tautology.
sourceAnswer: B. S1 is a tautology but S2 is not a tautology.

parsed.stem:
Let p and q be two propositions. Consider the following two formulae in propositional logic:

S1 : (~p -> q) -> q
S2 : ~q /\ (p \/ q) -> q

Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.text: Both S1 and S2 are tautologies.
S1 is a tautology but S2 is not a tautology.
S1 is not a tautology but S2 is a tautology.
Neither S1 nor S2 is a tautology.

sourceQuestion:
Choose the correct choice(s) regarding the following propositional logic assertion S:

S : ((P /\ Q) -> R) -> ((P /\ Q) -> (Q -> R))

A. S is neither a tautology nor a contradiction.
B. S is a tautology.
C. S is a contradiction.
D. The antecedent of S is logically equivalent to the consequent of S.

parsed.stem:
Choose the correct choice(s) regarding the following propositional logic assertion S:

S : ((P /\ Q) -> R) -> ((P /\ Q) -> (Q -> R))

options.text: S is neither a tautology nor a contradiction.
The antecedent of S is logically equivalent to the consequent of S.

sourceQuestion:
Let p and q be the following propositions:
p : Fail grade can be given.
q : Student scores more than 50% marks.

Consider the statement: 'Fail grade cannot be given when student scores more than 50% marks.'

Which one of the following is the CORRECT representation of the above statement in propositional logic?

A. q -> ~p
B. ~q -> p
C. p -> ~q
D. ~p -> q

parsed.stem:
Let p and q be the following propositions:
p : Fail grade can be given.
q : Student scores more than 50% marks.

sourceQuestion:
Let z and g be two propositions. Which of the following statements is a tautology / are tautologies?

A. (z /\ g) -> (z -> g)
B. (z /\ ~g) -> (~z -> g)
C. (~z /\ g) -> (z -> g)
D. (z /\ g) -> (g -> z)

(Select all that apply.)

parsed.stem: Let z and g be two propositions. Which of the following statements is a tautology / are tautologies?

sourceQuestion:
Let p, q, r and z be four primitive statements. Consider the following arguments:

P: [(p v q) ^ (p -> r) ^ (q -> r)] -> r
Q: [(p -> q) ^ (p -> ~q)] -> ~p
R: [(p -> q) ^ ~p] -> ~q
S: [(p -> q) -> r] -> (q -> r)

Which of the above arguments are valid?

A. P and Q only
B. P and R only
C. P and S only
D. P, Q, R and S

parsed.stem:
Let p, q, r and z be four primitive statements. Consider the following arguments:

P: [(p v q) ^ (p -> r) ^ (q -> r)] -> r
Q: [(p -> q) ^ (p -> ~q)] -> ~p
R: [(p -> q) ^ ~p] -> ~q
S: [(p -> q) -> r] -> (q -> r)

Which of the above arguments are valid?

sourceQuestion:
Let R denote the set of real numbers. Let f : R -> R -> R be a bijective function defined by g(C) = {f(x) | x in C} for all subsets C of R. The inverse function of f is given by

A. f^(-1)(x, y) = (x - y, x + y)
B. f^(-1)(x, y) = (x - y, x + y) / 2
C. f^(-1)(x, y) = ((x + y)/2, (x - y)/2)
D. f^(-1)(x, y) = (2(x - y), 2(x + y))

sourceQuestion:
The number of functions from an m element set to an n element set is

A. m + n
B. m^n
C. n^m
D. m * n

parsed.stem: The number of functions from an m element set to an n element set is

sourceQuestion:
Let f : A -> B be a function, and let E and F be subsets of A. Consider the following statements about images.

S1: f(E ∩ F) = f(E) ∩ f(F)
S2: f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F)

Which of the following is true about S1 and S2?

A. Only S2 is correct
B. Only S1 is correct
C. Both S1 and S2 are correct
D. None of S1 and S2 is correct

parsed.stem: Let f : A -> B be a function, and let E and F be subsets of

options.text: Consider the following statements about images. S1: f(E ∩ F) = f(E) ∩ f(F) S2: f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F) Which of the following is true about S1 and S2? A. Only S2 is correct
Both S1 and S2 are correct
None of S1 and S2 is correct

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following sets, where n >= 2:
  S_1: Set of all n x n matrices with entries from the set {a, b, c}
  S_2: Set of all functions from the set {0, 1, 2, ..., n^2-1} to the set {a, b, c}
Which of the following choice(s) is/are correct?
A. There does not exist a bijection from S_1 to S_2
B. There exists a surjection from S_1 to S_2
C. There exists a bijection from S_1 to S_2
D. There does not exist an injection from S_1 to S_2

options.text: There does not exist a bijection from S_1 to S_2
There exists a surjection from S_1 to S_2
There exists a bijection from S_1 to S_2
There does not exist an injection from S_1 to S_2

sourceQuestion:
Let f : A -> B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ~ on the set A as:
  a_1 ~ a_2 if f(a_1) = f(a_2)
where a_1, a_2 in A. Let E = {[a] | a in A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ~. Define a new mapping F : E -> B as
  F([a]) = f(a)   for all equivalence classes [a] in E.
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
A. F is NOT well-defined.
B. F is an onto (or surjective) function.
C. F is an injective function.
D. F is a bijective function.

parsed.stem:
Let f : A -> B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ~ on the set A as:
  a_1 ~ a_2 if f(a_1) = f(a_2)
where a_1, a_2 in

options.text: Let E = {[a] | a in A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ~. Define a new mapping F : E -> B as F([a]) = f(a) for all equivalence classes [a] in E. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? A. F is NOT well-defined.
F is an onto (or surjective) function.

sourceQuestion:
Let A and B be non-empty finite sets such that there exist one-to-one and onto functions (i) from A to B and (ii) from A x A to A union B. The number of possible values of |A| is ___________.
A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

parsed.stem: Let A and B be non-empty finite sets such that there exist one-to-one and onto functions (i) from A to B and (ii) from A x A to A union B. The number of possible values of |A| is ___________.

sourceQuestion:
Suppose L = {p, q, r, s, t} is a lattice represented by the following Hasse diagram:

(Hasse diagram: t is the top element; p and q are below t; r is below p; s is below q; r and s both connect to a bottom element, and p and q share certain meets/joins.)

For any x, y in L, not necessarily distinct, x v y and x ^ y are join and meet of x, y respectively. Let L^3 = {(x,y,z) : x,y,z in L} be the set of all ordered triplets of the elements of L. Let p_r be the probability that an element (x,y,z) in L^3 chosen equiprobably satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z). Then

A. p_r = 0
B. p_r = 1
C. 0 < p_r <= 1/2
D. 1/2 < p_r <= 1

parsed.stem:
Suppose L = {p, q, r, s, t} is a lattice represented by the following Hasse diagram:

(Hasse diagram: t is the top element; p and q are below t; r is below p; s is below q; r and s both connect to a bottom element, and p and q share certain meets/joins.)

For any x, y in L, not necessarily distinct, x v y and x ^ y are join and meet of x, y respectively. Let L^3 = {(x,y,z) : x,y,z in L} be the set of all ordered triplets of the elements of L. Let p_r be the probability that an element (x,y,z) in L^3 chosen equiprobably satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z). Then

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following Hasse diagrams.

i. A chain: a < b < c (total order on 3 elements)
ii. A diamond: bottom, two incomparable middle elements, top (M2 / B2)
iii. A pentagon: bottom, then two levels with a side branch (N5 — the pentagon lattice)
iv. A 3-element antichain with a top and bottom element added

Which all of the above represent a lattice?

A. (i) and (iv) only
B. (ii) and (iii) only
C. (iii) only
D. (i), (ii) and (iv) only

parsed.stem:
Consider the following Hasse diagrams.

i. A chain: a < b < c (total order on 3 elements)
ii. A diamond: bottom, two incomparable middle elements, top (M2 / B2)
iii. A pentagon: bottom, then two levels with a side branch (N5 — the pentagon lattice)
iv. A 3-element antichain with a top and bottom element added

Which all of the above represent a lattice?

sourceQuestion:
A line L in a circuit is said to have a stuck-at-0 fault if L permanently has a logic value 0. Similarly, L is said to have a stuck-at-1 fault if L permanently has a logic value 1. A circuit is said to have a multiple fault if one or more lines have stuck-at faults. The total number of distinct multiple faults possible in a circuit with N lines is:
A. 3^N - 1
B. 3^(N-1)
C. 2^N - 1
D. 2

A circuit is said to have a multiple fault if one or more lines have stuck-at faults. The total number of distinct multiple faults possible in a circuit with N lines is:

sourceQuestion:
In how many ways can n blue balls and m red balls be distributed in k distinct boxes such that each box contains at least one ball of each colour?
A. C(n-1, k-1) * C(m-1, k-1)
B. (n+m)! / ((n-1)! * (m-1)!)
C. n! / (k! * (n-k)!) * m! / (k! * (m-k)!)
D. C(n+m-1, k-1)

parsed.stem: In how many ways can n blue balls and m red balls be distributed in k distinct boxes such that each box contains at least one ball of each colour?

sourceQuestion:
The number of substrings (of all lengths inclusive) that can be formed from a character string of length n is:
A. n
B. n^2
C. n(n+1)/2
D. 2^n

parsed.stem: The number of substrings (of all lengths inclusive) that can be formed from a character string of length n is:

sourceQuestion:
There are 6 jobs with distinct difficulty levels, and 3 computers with distinct processing speeds. Each job is assigned to a computer such that:
- The fastest computer gets the toughest job and the slowest computer gets the easiest job.
- Every computer gets at least one job.
The number of ways in which this can be done is ______.
A. 3
B. 5
C. 65
D. 15

parsed.stem:
There are 6 jobs with distinct difficulty levels, and 3 computers with distinct processing speeds. Each job is assigned to a computer such that:
- The fastest computer gets the toughest job and the slowest computer gets the easiest job.
- Every computer gets at least one job.
The number of ways in which this can be done is ______.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a matrix P whose only eigenvectors are the multiples of [1, 4]^T. Consider the following statements.
I. P does not have an inverse
II. P has a repeated eigenvalue
III. P cannot be diagonalized
Which one of the following options is correct?
A. Only I and III are necessarily true
B. Only II is necessarily true
C. Only I and II are necessarily true
D. Only II and III are necessarily true
sourceAnswer: D. Only II and III are necessarily true

parsed.stem:
Consider a matrix P whose only eigenvectors are the multiples of [1, 4]^T. Consider the following statements.
I. P does not have an inverse
II. P has a repeated eigenvalue
III. P cannot be diagonalized
Which one of the following options is correct?

options.text: Only I and III are necessarily true
Only I and II are necessarily true
Only II and III are necessarily true

M = [[-9,-6,-2,-4],[-8,-6,-3,-1],[20,15,8,5],[32,21,12,7]]

    | 1  2  3 |

is

A. -1
B. 0
C. 1
D. 2
